title: The rate limit that rejected the launch
tagline: Support is drowning in 429 screenshots while your checkout fleet naps at 13% CPU.
brief: You front the storefront with an API gateway: rate limiting, auth, then straight to the Checkout API fleet and Postgres. During last month's load test someone capped the gateway at 400 req/s 'temporarily' to protect a half-built backend — and never reverted it. This morning marketing launched the spring drop. Legitimate traffic is 2 000 req/s. The gateway is doing exactly what it was told.
symptom: Watch the Edge Gateway's error rate pin at 80% and the log warning 'Rate limiting active. 80% requests rejected.' Meanwhile the Checkout API behind it idles around 13% CPU — a 30-instance fleet built for 3 000 req/s, receiving 400. Every one of those rejections is a paying customer seeing a 429.

phases.description: Launch steady state — 2 000 req/s of legitimate checkout traffic

schemaMemory.aiNotes: Write-heavy order store behind the checkout path.

diagnosis.question: The gateway rejects 80% of requests from the first second of the run, while everything behind it is idle. What's the root cause?

options.text: rateLimitRps (400) is set far below the legitimate peak of 2 000 req/s — the gateway itself is shedding real customers.
options.explain: Right. The gateway passes the first 400 req/s and rejects the rest: (2 000 − 400) ÷ 2 000 = 80%, exactly the error rate on its card. A rate limit is meant to cap callers ABOVE your legitimate peak; set below it, the limiter becomes the outage.

options.text: The Checkout API is saturated and the gateway is shedding load to protect it — adaptive backpressure working as designed.
options.explain: Open the Checkout API card: ~13% CPU, zero errors — there is nothing to protect. This limiter is a static cap, not adaptive backpressure; it rejects everything above the configured 400 req/s regardless of how healthy the fleet behind it is.

options.text: It's a bot flood — the limiter is correctly filtering illegitimate traffic from the launch hype.
options.explain: The traffic profile is a flat 2 000 req/s of authenticated checkout requests with no step change or spike that would mark an attack. A correctly sized limiter trims the excess above your legitimate peak; this one turns away four of every five paying customers from t=0.

options.text: Postgres's connection pool is exhausted and the failures are bubbling up to the edge as 429s.
options.explain: Pool exhaustion would show as database error rate, 'Connection pool exhaustion' log lines, and climbing latency — Postgres here sits near 25% utilization with zero errors. Besides, the rejected requests never reach the database at all: the rejection count exactly matches arrivals minus the 400 req/s cap, so the decision is made at the gateway.

fix.objective: Let the legitimate 2 000 req/s through: the gateway should reject (almost) nothing, and the Checkout API has to absorb the full launch load without melting.
hints: The gateway rejects everything above rateLimitRps, full stop. Compare the configured limit against the 2 000 req/s arriving — what fraction has to be rejected?
Click the Edge Gateway node: rateLimitRps is the cap. Size it for legitimate peak with headroom — but first check what the fleet can absorb: 30 instances ÷ 10 ms = 3 000 req/s of capacity.
Set rateLimitRps to about 3 000 — the full 2 000 req/s peak plus 50% headroom, and exactly what the 30-instance Checkout fleet can serve.
